item.title: Angry
item.description: https://twitter.com/JaimePrimak/status/1358089885366771716 (originally posted by @Varaxis on 2021-02-13 15:39:31)
item.dc:creator: ThisTimeLastYear

item.title: Assorted All Video Dump
item.description:
Welcome to the dump! Happy Saturday everyone, hope you’re all having a great day wherever you are in the world! Let’s start the dump off with attempted to set an airplane on fire. Good a place as any I suppose. Buckle up, it’s about to get bumpy.
#5
#10 WAP
#15
Any golf nerds know what to do when there’s a birdie?
Birdie?
#20
Just like mommy
#25
#30
#35
#40
#45
Frustrated cat tax So what’d ya think? Decent dump? Waste of your time?
item.dc:creator: knowallknownothing

item.title: Thank you human for your kindness.
item.dc:creator: Rammsteinstochter

item.title: Building my end table, an album
item.description:
This is my end table. It also does other stuff. Scroll down for build album. Build video https://youtu.be/TPOxjTXQWDI Some answers to frequently asked questions: Yes, it is over-complicated, over-engineered, and far less practical than a TV tray set from Amazon. This was just meant to be a fun project. Plans - I got a lot of requests for plans. The plans I have are not in good enough shape to recreate this and it would be a significant effort to get them there. That said I'd like to gauge interest. If after reading through this build album and seeing what goes into this project you are still interested let me know in the comments or a DM. I may make plans if there's still enough interest. I have no intention to sell this piece or make more to sell. I estimate I spent over 1000 hours on the build, and several hundred more on the design. There are close to 100 individual parts. I am a hobbyist who works about 10-20 hours a week in my shop and much of the build time was spent fixing mistakes. I worked on and off on this project for close to 4 years. The total material cost is around $700 - $1000. This includes raw materials (plywood, lumber, veneer, metal and plastic) and hardware (bearings, shafts, dampers, springs, and nuts and bolts). I spent more than that because of screw-ups. The table can comfortably about 15 lbs at full extension, but I have tested it up to twice that. When fully clamped, it does still sway a bit at the shoulder. It's reasonably quiet. The counterweight can be adjusted to lift more weight. The whole thing weighs like 150lbs. If you're interested in following along with the next projects you can find me on Instagram https://www.instagram.com/free_range_walnut/ If you're in the Boston area and interested in metal working check out the Framingham Makerspace https://www.framinghammakerspace.org/
Some more features
This is a long album. I went into a lot of detail because there seems to be a fair amount of interest.
I designed the components as solid monolithic parts, often with integral hardware. As I built each piece I would figure out how I was going to make and glue together the individual subcomponents. This took a lot of thought.
The frame was reasonably low tolerance. +/- 1/32 in most cases. I marked the cuts and went to work with the router, drill press, chisels and hand tools.
Two matching L-shaped templates with a pattern bit are a very accurate way to cut multiple pass mortises I find. This mortise supports a bridge for the counterweight.
Getting my money's worth out of my drill press. Here I'm drilling the hole for one of the counterweight box guide rails.
Using a trammel jig to cut large radii.
I use a dial indicator mounted on a mag base to adjust my fence on the table saw. Get within 1/32", measure the feature with calipers and move the fence accordingly with the indicator. I can get +/- .001" precision this way when I need to.
Dry fitting as I go along to make sure I knew what I was doing. The square cutout on the bottom right gives a little extra length to the riser rails and adds some stability.
I left the internal radii from the router where possible, but some places needed sharp inside corners. It was a tight squeeze sometimes. And yes plywood is terrible on your tools.
One of the most used tools in my shop is a large 3" x 6" x 3' caul. It can be regularly jointed flat with a square edge. I used it regularly during glue-ups like here to make a flat lamination.
Practicing for IKEA try-outs.
Final'ish dry fit
The joinery is mainly dados and rabbets which do not fully define position so I use guide blocks for the final alignment. It makes for a more complicated glue-up but I feel like I have more control this way.
Eventually the top is glued in place, leaving only this side of the case open. To make a true torsion box that won't twist too much I added this cross member. Its position is adjustable to 'set' the amount of twist. Both sides have a strain relief so only axial forces are transmitted.
For some reason I added a curve to the corner of this. Cutting it on the case was relatively easy. Using the arc center from the plans I attached a pivot to the back of the case and swung the whole thing on the router table. UNFORTUNATELY that meant the top had to be cutting against the grain which had predictable results. I patched it later with epoxy.
Cheese
Done with the case. This process took about 6 weeks,
These are the rails that the whole system rides up and down on. 20mm with linear ball bearings. These are left over from an old CNC I made.
The bearing and rod support spacing required more precision than anything else on this project. The overall spacing (6") was pretty loose, but the consistency had to be within about .001" to run smoothly. I spent a lot of time on these parts.
I predrilled the holes for the rail and bearing supports undersized, then brought it to final diameter on the router with this jig. Registering a spacer block against the stop on the left provides consistent spacing on all four parts. The final diameter is transferred to the with a flush trim bit, and routers don't walk like drills.
Happy with the result.
On to the so-called riser carriage. It raises and lowers the tray and arms, and houses several components for breaking and DOF control. Here I'm cutting the lock cam profile. It's a circle positioned slightly eccentric to an arm that pulls the slack out of the system when it closes.
Taking my time to get good alignment using dry fits.
Final glue-up.
The riser carriage needs to support a panel that extends about twice it's profile so it gets an arm glued in.
A completed riser carriage ready to be populated.
Up until now it's been all wood, but not all's wood in love and war. Many of the parts are much more practically suited to metal, and some absolutely need to be metal. I joined my local makerspace to get access to metal working equipment like this lathe,
and this mill. They were indispensable in completing this project, and with the help of a few million Youtube hours I learned quite a bit.
Many of the parts that NEEDED to be metal were lock rings to attach components to shafts. I used pretty much the same recipe through out the project - lock collars roughed up and fluted in the direction of most force, epoxied into an oversized hole. These collars are for the barrel cam.
To make the cam body I cut the rough shape into an octagon and drilled a hole for the shaft. Even when drilling from both sides I had just barely enough quill to meet in the middle.
The shaft and screws keep the epoxy from going everywhere. I would remove them and clean with acetone periodically to keep it from sticking.
I built a dedicated jig to make this part. The cam is allowed to spin, slide axially, and lift on and off the router. First step was to make it round.
I 3D printed a template to transfer the curves. The buttresses here are for support only and get trimmed off. This was roughly a 30 hour print and it took be 3 tries to get right.
UNFORTUNATELY I left it in my car on a hot day and the sun warped the crap out of it. Rather than print another I decided to salvage the top part as seen here and change plans for the rest.
The bottom part was just lines and a radius which is manageable with flat templates and an end stop. It was critical to get the phase right between the vertical slot and the little ramp on the upper slot. These features allow the shoulder brake knob to hide beneath the upper arm when being lowered inside the case.
Done.
Skipping ahead a bit here to show the cam in action.
This is the shoulder brake transfer block. The hole spacing of the rails and acme nut needed to be precise so I used the hole spacing jig from the linear rail mounts. The spacing block on the lower left and a feeler gauge provide an acceptable position tolerance.
As the block is pushed down by the screw, the two bearings squeeze a brake caliper connected to the shoulder shaft.
This is how I cut small circles. The part is held in place with a flat head screw and rotated with the edge of a push block against the bit. The whole thing rotates on and off the router via the pivot seen on the right of the table. The diameter is adjusted by moving the stop and the depth of cut is measured using my dial indicator wama-zama.
Takes a bit of getting used to but I find it rather safe.
Nice and round and to size. This holds the bearing for the shoulder brake knob and plugs the corresponding hole.
It gets attached to this L-shaped thing that is driven up and down by the barrel cam.
Dry fitting as I go along.
CAD for a slip joint for the shoulder brake. Metric bros will say it's fake.
The slip joint allows the knob to connect to the shoulder brake screw while still moving up and down.
A few simple lock collars for the acme screw and the shoulder brake assembly is done.
The shoulder brake shaft. A beefy roller thrust bearing and washer pair hides in the counterbore at the bottom.
The L-shaped thing ties into the barrel cam with this bracket. It's screwed on with oversized holes to give some adjustability.
The brake caliper is an odd shape and perfectly suited for 3D printing. It sits on a Teflon pad.
An almost fully assembled riser carriage. The horizontal member at the top is adjustable to level the sub-sub-top with the sub-top.
One more part is needed to define the basic motion of the riser. This bearing block mounts to the case and ties into the barrel cam. It defines the highest and lowest positions of the riser, prevents it from falling back down when the arms are rotated out, and aligns the shoulder as it descends into the table.
With the bearing block in place, I could finally test the cam. Worked right away to my surprise. Definitely a validating moment.
Moving on from the bowels of the case it was time to start the arms. More Baltic Birch assemblies. The main body is a very strong torsion box but the joint connections always worried me. There is a lot of load and plywood is very limited in taking screws. I did my best but time will tell... Pictured here is the upper arm.
The upper shoulder shaft seat. The small lip on the inside takes the thrust load.
The upper seat was a close fit to the shaft (~.001 clearance) but the lower seat was oversized by 1/16". A ring of 4 set screws allows for leveling.
Looks like a bug.
The elbow pin is fixed to the upper arm with two steel collars in a clevis arrangement.
I reamed to a precise fit with sandpaper wrapped around a pin. You can see 2 of the three shims used to center the collar in the bore during epoxying.
The center of the collar relative to the rest of the block was not precise so I shaved off a little material from the mating surfaces until the pin was decently square to the arm.
More fun clamping. Guide blocks were essential to keep things perpendicular.
I think it looks like a train now.
This is the internal structure of the forearm. The long "slots" on either side house the clamps for the wrist and elbow joints.
Gluing on the side walls. You know what they say about clamps...
Once the top and bottom were glued on, I cut a router template to create the bearing bores. I used a forstner bit to make the hole, then some tape to bring the diameter down enough for a light press fit.
I rotated the wrist end around a shaft through the bearings on the router table to create the radius. The far side radius was manageable enough to be cut with the bearing template pictured earlier.
The elbow and wrist clamps use a 3D printed caliper to squeeze a disk. A knob pulls the far ends together to clamp.
The arms fit together, nice.
I got most of the specialty hardware and metal stock from McMaster-Carr. There are cheaper options, but their site is amazingly organized and easy to order from.
Back to the machine shop to make the wrist bracket. I chose 4140 annealed and mic 6 jig plate.
Lot's of roughing with a carbide end mill.
I cut the main reference face and the hole for the shaft in one setup to maximize perpendicularity. I bored the hole with the matching bracket in place, then skimmed off some material at the interface so it could clamp.
Being the vain hipster that I am I decided to surface grind the whole part to make it shiny. But since I'm not very good at it I warped the part substantially. Rather than make a whole new one I decided to true it up on the lathe. I mounted the shaft in the chuck and the bracket on the shaft and took a face cut. I could then go back to the mill and use this surface to recreate my other references. And yes I did surface grind it again after a few more dozen hours of youtube.
Reround and added the aluminum plate that attaches to the top. It's a lot of bracket and I think if I did this again I would have made it a weldment. Just have to learn how to weld first....
Now that I knew the arms worked it was time to come back to the internals. These are the parts for the counterweight box. I don't have a picture for some reason but just imagine a box.
The counterweight is attached to the riser through a pully routed over a bridge in the rear of the cabinet. The extent of travel of the riser combined with the size of the counterweight left very little room for the bridge. Given the loads involved I went the safe route and used aluminum. Pictured here is the riser side where one of the pulleys sits.
Zing
The lock mechanism involved a lot of machined parts. This is the spring cable mount for the locking arm. By rotating the part around the pin and feeding it under the mill you can develop a pretty precise and concentric circle.
Roughing out the arm that connects the spring and a small damper to the locking arm shaft.
All cleaned up. I put multiple connection points so I could change the spring load and the damping torque. They would prove unnecessary but they look cool I think.
The fat pulleys are for the counterweight. The skinny pully is for the locking arm spring cable.
iT wOrKeD iN sOlIdWoRkS, except it didn't, because I didn't leave enough clearance for the riser side pulley. A tight squeeze with the router made for a quick fix.
Lock arm assembly in place. The spring pulls the lock arm into place and the damper keeps it smooth.
The locking system in action. The eccentricity is oriented such that there is a gradually decreasing downward push which eventually ends in a toggle. This pulls the slack out of the system so the top is sitting securely on the case.
I went with the pulley because there was more room this way and I had more freedom to adjust the spring.
One of many times assembling or disassembling this thing. I don't remember which I was doing in this picture.
This is the handle that releases the riser. It takes a fair amount of torque so I maximized the size of the steel insert. Like the others it's roughed up nicely on the grinder. The small screw hole is only there to hold it in place while the epoxy cures. I cut the mortise in the handle when it was a large flat block of wood, then shaped the handle around the mortise. Much easier and safer this way.
Moving on the (lower) top. This is the secondary top that's exposed when you lift the TV tray part off. This was also my first attempt at veneering. I found it to be a steep learning curve so what better to learn on than a project I've sunk hundreds of hours into already.
I bought a starter kit with the pump and bag from Vacuum Pressing Systems. Very happy with the quality and they have lots of good instructional videos too.
Veneered with the arm hole roughed out. Later I would clean it up with a router and chisels. My goal was to keep grain continuity across the riser top but I screwed up the order of the veneers and had to abandon that early on.
I weighed lots of options for attaching the top. Ultimately either the riser top or the sub-top have to be adjustable so they mesh nicely. I decided to make the sub-top fixed so I could seamlessly paint over the edge. Here I am gluing it onto the case. Lots of clamps, cauls, and concrete.
When cutting nested panels like this I shoot for about .005" gap. This gives some room for the varnish, and a little extra because nobody's perfect.
Next I made the riser top. I needed a precise hole for the shoulder clamp boss so I made a template in aluminum and flush trimmed it.
Eye added a 10 degree chamfer to the bottom of the knob hole so the plug could slide in easily. The chamfer extends almost all the way to the top of the panel to minimize any friction between the two pieces. There is a similar chamfer on the large cutout for the arms.
Moving on to the side door, or drawer door, or gull wing cabinet as I called it when I was designing it because it sounds cool but I know that's not technically what it is. Anyway. There's a sizable torsion box in the rear that keeps the whole thing very ridged. I went with wooden drawer slides mounted to the case and slots in the drawers. The long slots here accept said drawer slides and the short ones are mortises for the cross members.
Hardware for the door hinges. The bronze bearings are pressed into the brackets which are then mounted above and below the door in pockets on the frame. Both brackets are adjustable +/- 1/32" in each direction to center the door in the cutout.
The hinge pins are spring loaded out but slide flush into the door for installation. A bolt-action arrangement keeps them locked in place (kinda). Installing the springs was a bit of a challenge. Needle nose pliers eventually did the trick.
A tape shim for the haters. This evenly spaces the vertically door in the side cutout.
Finally time for some real woodworking when it was time to make the drawers.
Drawer stock roughed out. I took the easy road here and just made square boxes with curved fronts. The fronts were just barely too tall for my bandsaw so I roughed them out on the table saw with a bunch of passes.
At 4" high, the drawer fronts curve was too much to do as a profile pass with my equipment so I opted for a face cut instead. The workpiece gets double-sided taped to the board underneath the handle and I work my way over the whole surface.
Before and after the jig.
The drawer handles are glued on with dowels. I drilled the dowel holes first in the drawer fronts, then clamped the handles on and transferred the holes from the back side.
Drawer boxes were pretty standard. I cut my dove tails with a router jig. Don't tell Paul Sellers.
Fitting the drawers was a real challenge. The wood slides did not slide very well. I think I'd go with a piston fit style next time.
I like to uses flexures in place of a spring where possible. Here I used it on the drawer door lock catch.
The button, made of my usernamesake wood, is hidden on the "roof" of the lowest drawer.
The strike plate is mounted into oversized holes so I can adjust the location relative to the latch.
Polished the head of a bolt to make a stop for the door. It's adjustable to take the slack out of the latch. A corresponding furniture pad on the door makes for a soft-ish landing.
Knob time. I screwed up this one so I was able to get a cool cutaway view and reuse the shaft. The small bore at the end of the hole centers the shaft in the counterbore which is then filled with epoxy to make for a very secure joint. I use regular 2 part epoxy from Home Depot.
After the shaft is mounted I true up the knob on my OSHA-lathe.
Rounding the knob.
The bearing block is mounted with a single screw so I can cut a variety of angles
Post OSHA-lathe. I run the drill counter-clockwise to prevent a gearing situation.
Bling Bling. I added the low-poly look at the last minute for fun.
In this state the case weighed around 100 lbs. If I was too lazy to disassemble it I had to get creative to move it around.
Top veneer edge-glued prior to vacuum pressing. This solved some spreading issues I was having.
Nothing precise here. I roughed out the center and worked my way around the edges with a flush trim bit and guide. This is on the bottom so I was OK with predrilling the corner reliefs. There is only about 3/32" of material left but it will be partially supported by the bracket after installation.
I have nothing against CNCs, but I made it this far without one and wanted to see if I could finish it that way. This is a jig to cut the curved trim for the top panel. I cut it close to size and glued it to a piece of MDF to hold it.
I slid the jig across the flat part then rotated it around a pin for a precise tangent transition. To cut the mating profile on the veneered panel I offset the length of the arc by the length of the router bit. The bungee cord adds some stability.
Nice.
After assembling and testing all the parts it was time to take the whole thing apart for finish. There were a few parts I remade for the final assembly for one reason or another.
Family picture.
I wanted a solid continuous finish for the painted parts so I started with two coats of joint compound to even everything out. Way harder than it looked.
Next was a coat of primer.
The case, arms and back door were all painted.
The sub-top is maple veneer right up to the outside of the case, which is paint. I finished the top after painting the outside so I could sand a clean transition.
I like the unfinished look on the inside to match the rest of the components so I masked before painting.
Painting took forever. Especially since it was getting cold the days were getting shorter and I'm bad at painting.
All the non painted wood components are maple. I like a clear finish for this species so I used water based polyurethane. Everything gets 3 coats. The masked stripe here will give a little extra glue surface for the handles.
All finished finishing. Now I just have to remember how to put it back together....
All the wood parts.
The riser carriage subassembly.
There was some cleanup work after unmasking to get the hardware to fit. The lighting in my shop makes the paint look blue sometimes.
Installing the elbow pin is a rather clumsy operation to get all the thrust washers aligned. There are 3 top and bottom, 2 would be better. I blame the designer.
The counterweight filler - old ankle weight inserts, steel scraps and the pennies I've been saving since I was a kid for some reason.
Zing.
Zing part 2. The piston is an adjustable damper to control the ascent of the riser carriage.
Ready to move out of the shop. With the top, door, and counterweights removed the case weighs about 60lbs. Just barely light enough for me to carry up the stairs to our apartment.
Went heavy with the furniture pads. They are located at all the structural points.
Reggie approves.
And that's it. Time to put the jigs away and move on to the next project. Thanks for reading. Cheers.
I made lots of mistakes along the way. Most I fixed and many I just lived with. Here are some of them..
Paint never really came out great, probably from painting in the dark and cold.... I disassembled it enough to remask it and set up a makeshift paint booth in my shop.
This center panel in the case glued up crooked. I took a long cut out of it and glued in a shim to square it up.
I am not good at dovetails. I made this jig to hone them so they fit. It worked OK.
Didn't have a 3/8" shank bit but I had a 3/8" adapter...
I accidentally cut the first mortise in the space between where the actual mortises were supposed to go. Don't forget to mark your waste area...
I forgot to account for the extra room the forearm brake knobs needed as they swung closed into the cutouts on the upper arm. A sketchy flush trim jig on the router table cleaned them up.
Drilled all the way though this bearing bore and had to glue in the support after the fact.
Solidworks moment. Don't forget to plan for tool access. This one was not planned.
The wrist brake knob relief was still not big enough. A bunch of primer settled at the bottom. So I sanded it down even more and repainted just that area.
My poor veneering skills coming back to haunt me. After the first coat of finish the veneers started to peel up at the seams. I angrily routed them out and patched them. Not my finest work but it stabilized it enough.
Not perfect but good enough for Ben. This concludes this album.

item.title: I've been drawing nothing but Ladies in Armor for the past few weeks and now I'm going to ask politely for you to look at them. LOOK AT THEM
item.description:
Seen any elves? AHAHAHAHA
Gundrun "The Kraken", warrior princess
16th century over-the-top cuirass
Finnish mercenary during the viking age
Hussar from the Krajina
item.dc:creator: Callaxes

item.title: Day 260: of cheeseburgers I want inside of me
item.description: Bacon Cheeseburger On Steroids Beef Patty, Monterey Jack Cheese, Three Pieces Of Jalapeño Bacon, Three Pieces Of Applewood Smoked Bacon, Housemade Bacon Mayo, Shredded Iceberg Lettuce, Tomato, Red Onion & Pickles Bad Daddy’s Burger Bar In Aurora, CO Previous Day 259: https://imgur.com/gallery/mKXMWNE MESSAGE ME BURGER PICS OR YOUR FAVORITE BURGER SPOTS! Map of all burger spots that are available on yelp! Big thanks to @OhBoyDoIHaveNews4U for this. Link to his post and map https://imgur.com/gallery/vcObDuD
item.dc:creator: crypto

item.title: Wholesome!
item.description: That is.. Frontpage! Thank you guys *hugs and kisses*
item.dc:creator: IthinkIneedahug

item.title: Sometimes you just need some snacks
item.description:
After winning her second Olympic gold medal in the halfpipe snowboarding event, Chloe Kim was a little snackish…ask and you shall receive!
Throwback to the last Olympics when she tweeted *from the top of the run before winning gold* that she was getting hangry
